skill.name: ubiquitous-language
skill.description: Extracts a domain-driven design glossary from the current conversation, flags ambiguous terms, and saves canonical language to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md.
skill.summary: Create a focused ubiquitous language glossary from project discussion.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak crypto, and reconnaissance indicators, but review found prose and Markdown examples in SKILL.md. The skill intentionally reads and writes a local UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md file, which is disclosed and bounded. No network access, prompt injection attempt, secret handling, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Intentional Local Glossary File Access
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to write UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md and read it on later runs. This is expected behavior, but it can overwrite a local project document.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read and write behavior is explicit and limited to one named Markdown file. Confidence is high because the relevant lines directly describe the local filesystem action.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick execution locations are Markdown syntax and inline examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak crypto and reconnaissance indicators are prose or table content with no operational effect.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside YAML prose, Markdown code fences, tables, and quoted dialogue. No executable script, network call, shell invocation, or cryptographic API is present.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when one term means different things across product, design, and engineering. This skill turns conversation context into a clear ubiquitous language glossary.
actual_capabilities: Extracts domain terms from the current conversation.
Flags ambiguous, overloaded, and duplicate terminology.
Proposes canonical terms and aliases to avoid.
Groups terms into clear Markdown glossary sections.
Adds relationships between domain concepts when evident.
Writes or updates UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the working directory.
limitations: It only uses terms available in the current conversation.
It does not verify terms with real domain experts.
It writes one named Markdown file in the working directory.
It does not replace full domain modeling workshops.

use_cases.title: Clarify Product Requirements
use_cases.description: Convert discovery notes into shared terms before writing requirements or user stories.

use_cases.title: Align Domain Models
use_cases.description: Create canonical names for entities, lifecycles, and relationships before implementation.

use_cases.title: Reduce Team Miscommunication
use_cases.description: Document terms that customer support, design, and engineering must use consistently.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Glossary
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request after a planning conversation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ubiquitous language glossary from this conversation and save it to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve Ambiguous Terms
prompt_templates.scenario: Team members use the same word in different ways.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the glossary and clearly flag any ambiguous terms, synonyms, or overloaded concepts.

prompt_templates.title: Group by Domain Area
prompt_templates.scenario: The discussion covers several business areas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build the glossary with separate sections for each subdomain, lifecycle, or actor group.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Glossary
prompt_templates.scenario: The team added new context after an earlier glossary.
prompt_templates.prompt: Read UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md, incorporate the new discussion, and update definitions, aliases, relationships, and example dialogue.

output_examples.input: A checkout discussion uses customer, buyer, account, order, purchase, and invoice inconsistently.
output_examples.output: The output is a Markdown glossary with canonical terms, avoided aliases, concept relationships, and flagged ambiguity for account.

output_examples.input: A logistics team discusses shipment, fulfillment, delivery, dispatch, and cancellation rules.
output_examples.output: The output separates lifecycle terms, defines each concept in one sentence, and includes a short domain expert dialogue.

output_examples.input: An existing glossary needs updates after a new onboarding workflow discussion.
output_examples.output: The output revises UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md with new terms, changed definitions, new ambiguities, and an updated example dialogue.

best_practices: Run it after a focused domain discussion with concrete examples.
Review flagged ambiguities with a domain expert before adopting terms.
Keep the generated glossary in version control with related requirements.
anti_patterns: Using it on a conversation without enough domain context.
Treating proposed canonical terms as final without team review.
Including generic programming terms unless they have business meaning.

faq.question: What file does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates or updates UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the current working directory.

faq.question: Does it execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The shell-like tokens are Markdown examples, not executable code.

faq.question: Does it use external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed skill file contains no network access instructions.

faq.question: Can it validate terminology with customers?
faq.answer: No. It organizes conversation context, but humans must validate the glossary.

faq.question: Is it only for domain-driven design?
faq.answer: It is designed for DDD, but it can help any team standardize domain vocabulary.

faq.question: Can it update an existing glossary?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the agent to read the existing file and incorporate new terms.
